data.title: Fed Chair Powell Is Under Attack - Silver And Gold At Fresh All-Time Highs (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Donald Trump is a stubborn President, to say the least. Just when markets were enjoying a few relaxing months of silence regarding Fed independence, the Administration has struck again.

Over the weekend, the Department of Justice opened a formal investigation into Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell regarding statements made during his recent Senate testimony.

It is painfully easy to read between the lines here. This appears to be a political masquerade - a manufactured pretext to fire Powell before his term officially concludes.

The impatience seems unjustified, given that his tenure ends in May 2026 and markets are already expecting an announcement on his successor by the end of this month.

In a special address delivered yesterday evening, Chair Powell fired back, significantly upping his tone regarding the Federal Reserve's stance against politicized interference.

The verdict: market chaos.

The US dollar is plummeting to start the week, and stocks are in the red. On the other hand, keeping with the dominant trend of the past year, metals are shining bright.

Acting as the preferred safe haven - displacing even the yen and the now-compromised US Treasuries - precious metals have shot through the roof in early trading.

This political theater highlights a structural shift: Central Banks and investors are aggressively diversifying away from an overdependence on US assets as their traditionally stable reputation erodes.

Gold has now solidified its place as the largest reserve asset for central banks, with China leading a global diversification charge.

With silver breaking $85 and gold comfortably trading above its previous peak ($4,600+), the message is clear: Metals are not looking to ease their rally anytime soon.

The buying frenzy is broad-based, with platinum, palladium, copper, and aluminum all chasing new monthly or yearly highs as we speak.

Let's dive right into a two-time frame intraday analysis for gold (XAUUSD:CUR) and silver (XAGUSD:CUR) to see where these flows could be heading.

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) Daily and 2H Charts

Daily Chart

After the past week closed above $4,500, technicals went right ahead to magnify the return of even more bullish fundamentals for the yellow metal.

Since August, the rally hadn't seen many retracements, but after the late-December correction, a strong upward channel is forming, with the latest bounce being used by bulls to reach the current all-time highs.

Easily blasting beyond the weekly divergence noted last week.

Currently at $4,630 and running, gold will face a small technical resistance at $4,666, from a key Fibonacci projection (1.618 from 2023 lows to mid-2025 levels).

If bulls breach the level, there won't be much until $5,000, which coincides with the top of the channel.

Let's take a closer look.

2H Chart and Technical Levels

Forming a tight bull channel on the intraday time frame, nothing but some overbought conditions seem to be stopping the rally in gold.

Reaching session highs at $4,630, the frantic buying is stalling a bit.

For pullbacks, aggressive pullback buying could take place at $4,590 (intraday channel lows).

Breaking the steep session channel would point to a retest of the previous all-time highs around $4,550.

Levels to watch for gold (XAUUSD:CUR) trading:

Resistance levels

  • $4,630 current session and all-time high
  • $4,660 to $4,670 potential resistance
  • Potential Mini-Resistance 2 $4,700-4,720
  • Top of daily channel and psychological level $5,000

Support levels

  • Intraday channel lows $4,590
  • Previous ATH pivot (as support) $4,500-4,550
  • Session lows previous ATH pivot (as support) $4,500-4,550
  • Major intraday support $4,400 and 4H 50-MA
  • December 31 mini-support $4,280
  • Weekly major pivot $3,950-4,000

Silver (XAGUSD:CUR) Daily and 2H Charts

Daily Chart

The action in XAG/USD is starting to now look insane.

Silver is leading its peers yet again in today's action, up around 8% in a huge bullish candle in today's action, easily breaching beyond the $85 psychological level, leaving more technical upside to the rally.

Also forming a daily channel, no resistance is emerging before the $88-89 Fibonacci area.

Except for any major fundamental change (like world peace or a return of Fed hikes), nothing is looking to stop the run.

Let's see what the intraday charts are telling us.

2H Chart and Technical Levels

With the action now stalling a bit after the tumultuous overnight/morning action, silver traders will be looking at two technical elements:

  • Whether bulls manage to fully break above the sideways channel (testing a breakout)
  • Or follow the steep tight bull channel from the current session, pointing to even more aggressive action.
    • Breaking below this one hints at a retest of the $80.00 momentum pivot.
    • Maintaining the momentum would easily guide the action to $88 and potentially more.

To put things into perspective, silver is up 77% since Williams' comments hinted at a December Fed cut!

Levels to watch for silver (XAGUSD:CUR) trading:

Resistance Levels:

  • $86.23 session and all-time highs
  • Potential Mini-Resistance 1 $87 to $89
  • Potential Mini-Resistance and Psychological Level 2 $90 to $92

Support Levels:

  • $82 to $84 previous ATH pivot (as support)
  • $80.00 momentum pivot
  • $75 to $77 minor support and channel lows
  • December 31 lows $70.00
  • Pre-FOMC major support $58.00-60 and 50-Day MA

Safe Trades, and a successful week!

Original Post

data.title: From Storms To Servers: Why Generac Holdings' Moment May Be Here
data.description:
Thesis

Generac Holdings Inc. (GNRC) is an American energy tech company in the S&P 500 (SP500) that designs and makes power generators and backup energy systems for homes, businesses, factories, and data centers. These systems help provide reliable electricity when the power grid is overloaded or goes down. All things considered, I’m bullish on the stock as it transitions from a cyclical residential generator maker to a strategic player in large-scale backup power at the nexus of rising grid stress and AI data-center demand.

Generac Holdings at the Intersection of AI and Grid Stress

I'm treating 35 gigawatt idea as the key piece that connects the rapid growth of AI data centers with why Generac has suddenly become important in the large diesel generator market. Energy Secretary Chris Wright basically said the U.S. already has around 35 GW of diesel backup sitting around at data centers, big box stores, and industrial sites and he compared that to roughly 35 nuclear plants worth of capacity. I really don’t see that as a prediction, but as a change in perspective.

Backup generators used to be an insurance policy. They sat idle until the grid went down, and the buyer cared almost entirely about uptime. Power demand is climbing faster than utilities can build new plants or hook up new grid connections. It’s no longer only about a data center developer meeting reliability requirements. Regulators and electric grid operators are also under pressure to handle peak electricity demand, because new power generation and grid interconnection projects are taking longer to come online than demand is growing.

Once you view backup power through that lens, Generac’s move into large-scale, megawatt diesel generation no longer feels like a stretch of what it was already doing. Yes, 2025 still reminded everyone that Generac’s residential business is cyclical. CEO Jagdfeld explained that:

Home standby and portable generator shipments grew sequentially in the quarter but were below seasonal expectations as a result of a power outage environment that was significantly below our long-term baseline average and the lowest third quarter of total outage hours that we’ve experienced since 2015.

Power outages are a major reason people buy home standby systems. When there are fewer outages, sales in that area slow down. That, of course, is a timing issue, not a sign that the business model is flawed. The key consideration is what’s unfolding within the various parts of the business.

Last quarter, the company’s commercial and industrial sales increased by 9% to $358 million, even as residential sales slowed. This growth also included early shipments of large units designed for data centers. On top of that, their backlog for these large megawatt generators doubled to $300 million, and they started shipping domestically in October.

They’re also building around it, take the new facility in Sussex, Wisconsin, and plan to add more than 100 jobs to expand C&I capacity tied to data center demand. That’s not something you would do if you thought this was just a one-quarter issue. It shows me that management is committing more resources to supply, which is the part of the market under the most pressure right now.

The Tradeoffs Behind the Opportunity

Caterpillar (CAT) is the established leader in this space and Bank of America estimates that it holds about a 42% share of the data center generator market. Cummins (CMI) and Rolls-Royce (RYCEY) remain a step behind. So I’m not exactly hand-waving the difficulty of taking share.

That kind of trust is hard to shake, and that’s why I pay attention to things like how much of the backlog turns into revenue, whether service capacity adequately supports the installed base, or whether lead times are getting shorter or longer. Baird’s recent upgrade followed that same thinking. Outside summaries pointed out that the large diesel opportunity could add about $400 million to $800 million in revenue during 2027 to 2028. In reality, this is a battle for market share where cautious purchasing behavior works in two directions. It slows down adoption of new or smaller suppliers, but once a buyer decides to bring in a second supplier to reduce lead-time risk, that supplier usually stays in place.

Wright’s 35-gigawatt framing shows that the market opportunity is not limited to building new data centers. It also includes the existing installed base, where government policy decisions begin to directly influence and increase demand. Argus reported that Wright pointed to emissions rules as the key constraint, noting that federal regulations limit some backup generators to just 50 hours per year of non-emergency operation.

The rules are already heading that way. Last year, the EPA put out guidance that spelled this out more clearly. Basically, if certain conditions are met, emergency engines can run under the 50-hour allowance when the grid is under stress. Argus also flagged growing grid stress from data centers. For example, PJM’s independent market monitor recently went so far as to ask federal regulators to pause new data center hookups until there’s enough capacity to serve them reliably. Under that sort of squeeze, a diesel backstop starts to look politically irresistible, even if, to be clear, some people aren't exactly thrilled about how it looks.

I’m not assuming this turns into some kind of free-for-all. If anything, unfortunately, more diesel run time predictably brings more local pollution and, along with it, more political friction, while the basic economics of diesel make it a weak solution for sustained generation. The opening, such as it is, lies instead in limited-hour coverage and short-term bridging, where, because speed and bankability seem to matter more than elegance, diesel can still muscle its way into the conversation.

GNRC’s Valuation

GNRC is currently valued at a blended P/E ratio of 23.22x, according to FAST Graphs. This blended number comes from two things. First, its historical “normal” P/E of 16.64x. Second, forward earnings estimates that assume roughly 19% annual earnings growth through 2028.

I think this higher valuation is reasonable because the company is changing where its growth comes from. Residential sales fell 13% last quarter, mainly because there were fewer power outages. However, demand on the commercial and industrial side is strong.

Meanwhile, analysts expect EPS to grow at an average rate of 18.9%, improving sharply from -9.96% in 2025 to 24.96% in 2026.

Based on these expectations, analysts estimate the stock’s fair value today at $127.85, while targeting a future price of $216.89. However, keep in mind, there are risks that could weaken this positive outlook like problems executing the expansion of Sussex capacity could lead to higher cash spending. At the same time, free cash flow, which is already converting at 80%, is under pressure due to ongoing investment needs.

Remember, competition's strong because CAT already controls a large share of the market, a pressure that would increase if Generac starts missing delivery timelines, especially during a slowing economy or if regulators push back harder on diesel emissions. Also, dilution's not an issue, but aggressive M&A could make it necessary if growth slows.

Final Takeaway

Rating: Buy. I think the asymmetry is attractive here. The downside's mostly about timing. Residential outages come in cycles, and capex plus working capital are a drag in the near term. But the upside looks structural. This one’s really for long-term holders. You have to be okay with some volatility. In return, you get exposure to data-center power infrastructure without paying hyperscaler multiples.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Pampa Energía: How Cheaper Oil And Venezuelan Supply Could Affect Its Operations
data.description:
Pampa Energía S.A. (PAM) is one of the key Argentine energy companies in the utilities and oil and gas sector. The plus that I can see in Pampa is its diversification, but its exposure to oil, especially with the higher CapEx projected for 2026 dedicated to this segment, exposes the company to some significant profitability challenges.

I want to make this clear because of the context of uncertainty, starting with the United States intervention in Venezuela. I have become more moderate with my expectations about oil because some Argentine companies, despite their large investments in the unconventional Vaca Muerta deposit, could be affected if the price of oil falls too much. I believe that a lower-than-expected price environment would force a faster reduction in production costs. These circumstances have convinced me to rate some Argentine energy companies as Hold, including Pampa, which I want to analyze today.

Pampa Energía's Business

Let's begin by analyzing Pampa's segments to focus on its important focus on oil. By seeing the Q3 2025 results, oil and gas represented sales for $308 million, versus its total sales for $519 million, which represents almost 60%. The segment's net income was negative by $9 million, while the average price of a barrel of oil in the quarter was $61.1 compared to $61.9 in the first nine months of 2025.

The other important part of its sales depends on electric power generation, at almost 35%, and is followed by the petrochemical sector. The power generation business remains a core and stable part of the business, with an installed capacity of 5.5 GW, holding a 15% market share in Argentina, similar to the position of Central Puerto S.A. (CEPU). The difference from Central Puerto is that Pampa has its most attractive engine in Vaca Muerta and Oil and Gas, unlike Central Puerto, which is 100% dedicated to generating electricity. In my opinion, Central Puerto is less exposed to the uncertainty of oil prices, and I have recently explained my positive outlook on the company in a previous article.

Overall, considering all segments, the results for the quarter, and even for 2025, were positive. During Q3, there was a YoY growth of 16% in its adjusted EBITDA. Net income showed a drop of 84%, but this was a circumstantial sharp move due to the payment of deferred taxes, as can be seen on page 9 of the earnings release. Meanwhile, the largest increase in sales was in oil and gas (+35%), making it the best segment of the quarter and showing that investments in Rincón de Aranda (a key block in Vaca Muerta) are paying off, especially in the midstream business.

The company has 61% of Argentina's midstream capacity, with more than 9,000 km of gas pipeline thanks to the co-control of Transportadora Gas del Sur S.A. (TGS). It's also the main company of the midstream in Vaca Muerta, and it has 10% of ownership stake in the VMOS project, a pipeline designed to transport crude oil from Vaca Muerta (550 kbpd) to the Atlantic Ocean, especially for export.

This is another key part of the business because foreign sales grew 70% YoY in the first nine months of 2025, versus 1% growth of the Argentine market. In other words, the export of crude oil from Vaca Muerta is a major revenue driver for Pampa, which is why its investment efforts will continue to focus on this field, especially in Rincón de Aranda and particularly in combination with the upstream business. Ergo, Pampa is not only a great gas and oil distributor, thanks to Rincón de Aranda, but it's also a great producer.

Why Is Rincón de Aranda So Important For Pampa Energía?

Among the most important factors that make Rincon de Aranda a key place for the company, is because the block is 100% owned by Pampa Energía. This means that everything generated there belongs 100% to the company, including cash flow, EBITDA, and even cost control. This doesn't happen with other companies that exploit Vaca Muerta and represents a differentiator.

Another key factor is its good infrastructure due to its geographical location, where other energy players in Argentina are close by, which helps lower production costs. On the other hand, I'm seeing a very large number of reserves. With 286 wells, inventories, and estimated reserves of 400 million barrels, production is assured for the company for a long time, so I am seeing strong long-term development.

This long-term development needs investments, as you know, to extract crude oil, even more so in the case of an unconventional deposit. The planned capital investments for 2026 in Rincón de Aranda are $750 million, which represents almost 75% of all CapEx from the last 12 months to Q3 2025, as can be seen on pages 7 and 12 of Investor Presentation.

That investment is already reflected in the results, with an EBITDA margin of 52% and a strong year-on-year increase in production of 220%, going from 5.4 kbbl/d to 17.3 kbbl/d. The goal is to reach 45 kbbl/d in 2027, which would imply to produce almost half of the 82,2 kboe/d gas (6 cubic meters of gas converted to 1 boe). For now, of the 99.5 kboe/d produced in Q3, the crude from Rincón de Aranda represents about 20% of the segment's production.

Regarding production efficiency, the goal is to reduce the cost per barrel to below $5 in 2027. The cost per barrel is another important part of the business, even more so in the context of oversupply that could be coming in the next few years, as I'll explain later. During Q3, the cost per barrel fell quarterly from $7.6 to $6.4 showing an improvement that allowed it to remain profitable in the segment, with an operating margin of 15% in the oil and gas segment, affected by cost of sales in 2025. In summary, Rincon de Aranda profitability is the great attraction for investors that are looking for strong long-term revenue drivers.

Oil Perspectives For 2026

I wanted to explain a little one of the reasons I believe has attracted investors to PAMP in the last few years, which is its incorporation into the development of Vaca Muerta and its strong upstream growth. This has made Pampa a very comprehensive energy company, putting a bet on each of Argentina's energy ventures: electricity generation and distribution, gas transportation alongside Transportadora de Gas del Sur, and oil exploration and production.

I believe that targeting the oil and gas segment was an interesting and successful move so far, but even so, I think that no company, however efficient, is exempt from the risks of dependence on a commodity. In this case, the price of oil and, therefore, the interaction between production and consumption, play a key role.

With the capture of Nicolás Maduro in Venezuela and the intervention of the United States, the outlook of experts in the sector is a downward movement in the price of oil. I had seen the downward projections before the Venezuela incident because the market is already facing a situation of oversupply. Now, with the real possibility that Venezuela starts to regularize its oil production, some projections point out that crude oil's price could fall below $50.

By the end of last year, the price had already fallen to $55, a level not seen since 2021. Therefore, I don't think a $50 environment is necessarily far off, especially when I look at recent supply and its projections. In the last two quarters of 2025, the supply was 2.5 million barrels per day at least, opening a visible gap between production and consumption, as you can see in the following chart.

Looking ahead to 2026, OPEC is expected to continue producing between 30 and 35 million barrels per day. Meanwhile, EIA estimates a price of $55 per barrel and a supply of 2 million barrels per day for 2026, a forecast made before the Venezuelan incident.

Strong global oil production growth has outpaced consumption in recent months, driving our assessment that global oil inventories have risen quickly in the second half of 2025. In 2026, we expect production and consumption to grow at similar rates, but production levels will continue to exceed consumption, further adding to inventories. We forecast that global oil inventory builds will exceed 2 million barrels per day (b/d) in 2026, which is similar to this year’s increase.

The Impact Of Venezuela's Possible Offer

Now, I'd like to add the surplus that Venezuela could contribute since the American intervention, which could increase the production in the mid-term (12 to 24 months) from 963,000 barrels per day to 1.5 million barrels per day. That will require the elimination of sanctions and investments of at least $10 billion.

If production increases, there will be a great supply potential because Venezuela's reserves are 364 billion oil barrels equivalents, while Argentina, for example, has only 3 billion of proven reserves. The Venezuela situation is basically about waking up a sleeping oil giant, which, by simply adding an extra 500,000 barrels per day, I believe will imply a new blow to the price given the current supply context.

But pay attention because the potential is even bigger if production increases quickly: in 2008 Venezuela was producing around 3 million barrels per day. Of course, I have no certainty about how future investments could develop in the sector, since they involve some significant challenges because there are approximately 28,000 inactive wells that need rehabilitation in mature regions. But what I'm sure of is the market is heading towards a greater global supply of oil than in 2025, and therefore towards lower prices.

This affects investment projects in the upstream segment of Pampa Energía, which have been the main attraction for investors in the last few years thanks to the development potential of Rincón de Aranda. A bigger oil supply puts pressure on margins and profitability, but I also don't think Pampa will halt its planned investments for the coming years and its production target of 45 kbbl/d by 2027. That's why I think the company is entering a context of uncertainty, in which I would prefer not to do any moves in the shares.

The Effects Of A Cheap Barrel

I want to show a chart I put together with Pampa's latest results, evaluating the first nine months of 2025 for the two main segments: oil and gas and power generation. This is where I see the difference between the average barrel price in the 2024 period, at $71, versus 2025, at $61.9.

The greatest impact I believe is on the operating income, which illustrates the challenge to business efficiency when the price of oil falls 14% YoY. In the same period, the costs per barrel grew 22%. If moderate projections of EIA come true and the price falls $55, the decline will be 9%. In summary, even in the best-case scenario of an average price of $55 per barrel in 2026, it will be a year of pressure on the company's efficiency.

On the other hand, I also want to mention that Pampa's stock is trading at high multiples compared to its historical average. The EV/EBITDA is 9.08x compared to 8.54x for its last 5 years, while the P/E is trading with an upward difference of 100.97%.

Domestic Risks

Beyond the external risks to Pampa, regarding the international oil context, the company also has some local risks, especially while operating in Argentina, which is a very volatile market. I want to show you an example of what happened during October 2025 legislative elections, where the ruling party won by a good margin against the opposition and temporarily dispelled the fear of the return of Peronism.

Currently, with President Javier Milei in power, the country is making great efforts to regain confidence in international markets with an economic policy based on fiscal surplus, lower inflation and, as a result, lower country risk, which is close to 570 bps.

Pampa Energía took advantage of the favorable post-election context to place debt at 7.75% for $450 million, positive news that raises confidence among investors. However, Argentina still has some challenges ahead, among them, I could mention the reforms that still need to be debated in Congress, now with an official majority, and the debt maturities that the country must face in 2026 and 2027. Any negative effect in these events, I believe, will have an impact on Pampa's shares, as well as Argentinian shares in general.

But I consider the greatest risks to be the global uncertainties about oil supply and prices in 2026, and the long-term perspectives. Large Argentine oil companies, such as YPF Sociedad Anónima (YPF) or Vista Energy, S.A.B. de C.V. (VIST), are more exposed to this uncertainty. But Pampa, although it doesn't dedicate 100% to oil, due to its exposure to expectations, investment, and revenue (the Oil and Gas segment represents 60% of sales), I believe will generate doubts in the short term. My position, then, is to wait. In case you already own Pampa's shares, I don't think this is the best time to buy more, nor do I believe that the position should be sold because the company remains profitable and attractive.

data.title: Synchrony Financial: Trump's Credit Card Proposal Creates An Opportunity
data.description:
Shares of Synchrony Financial (SYF) plunged 7% in early trading Monday as investors digested news over the weekend that President Trump wants to cap credit card interest rates at 10% for a year. As you can see below, this pullback comes after a strong run in shares, with SYF still up 26% from last year. Such an effort by the president is likely to face significant regulatory and legal hurdles, but it clearly ratchets up political pressure on credit card companies like Synchrony to reduce rates. I last covered shares of Synchrony in November, rating the stock a “Buy.” Even with Monday’s pullback, Synchrony is up 11% since then, justifying the rating. Given this new development, now is a good time to revisit SYF.

A 10% cap on credit card rates is unlikely

While the President says he wants to cap interest rates at 10% starting on Jan. 20, the legal authority to do so is questionable. Currently, the federal government only limits the rate charged on active-duty service members, which is set at 36%. Credit card rates are instead regulated at the state level; however, national banks are limited by the rate set in their home state. Synchrony is headquartered in the state of Delaware, which is the legal home of much of Corporate America given its lax regulatory framework and has no cap on credit card rates. It is very unclear what authority the President could use from the Executive Branch alone to enforce such a cap.

Congress could seek to pass a national interest rate cap, though it likely would take more than 3 weeks. Such a measure has drawn bipartisan support before, but I believe ultimate passage would be difficult, given likely strong opposition from the historic pro-business wing of the Republican party. In particular, there is likely to be significant concern that credit card issuers significantly reduce the credit offered to higher-risk borrowers, cutting the availability of credit and actually having a negative economic impact. Absent a formal cap, the Administration could certainly take a more aggressive regulatory and enforcement approach to credit card issuers to encourage them to reduce rates.

SYF's business model does not work with such a cap

While a credit card interest rate cap is not my base case, the risk of one occurring is clearly higher than when I last covered SYF, and at the very least, the regulatory backdrop is more onerous. This makes the negative reaction in shares quite understandable, especially considering the potential financial ramifications. In the company’s third quarter, SYF charged an average interest rate of 21.89% on its $100 billion of loans. This translated to ~$5.7 billion of interest income.

After credit losses, interest expense, and all other costs, Synchrony’s pre-tax income was $1.43 billion. At a 10% cap, SYF would lose about $3 billion of interest revenue in a quarter. All else equal, it would swing from a ~$1.43 billion pre-tax gain to a ~$1.57 billion pre-tax loss. Now, one mitigating factor is that SYF shares some of its earnings with its retail partners, based on the performance of its cards. With performance so dramatically worsening, its ~$1.02 billion in retail share arrangements would likely fall. Still, even if this was nearly eliminated, SYF would operate at a loss.

This is because while SYF charges ~22% for credit card loans, it earns far less than 22%. First, the company, like any bank, borrows to finance its loans. Its cost of interest-bearing liabilities is 4.2%, leaving it with a 15.62% net interest margin. Then, as noted above, it shares some economics with its retail partners, costing it ~4% of loans, for an ~11.6% margin. This margin is before any credit losses. Because credit cards are unsecured loans, they tend to be among the highest-loss financial products, and these losses are also quite cyclical (meaning they get worse in a recession).

We are currently in a relatively normal credit loss environment. As you can see below, net charge-offs were 5.16% of loans in Q3. Over the past two years, they have ranged from 4.60% to 6.42%. While they can rise substantially during a recession, I view 5-6% as the long-run norm for Synchrony’s business. This means that after credit losses, SYF’s net margin is ~6%.

This is why a ~10% cap is not really workable for the credit card business and would be so devastating for a company like Synchrony. In theory, a ~16-17% cap would more likely represent the financial breakeven because of the other costs of financing the business. Potentially assuming a lower negotiated retail share, that could be closer to ~14%, but that would merely be breakeven, which is not sustainable in the long term. This reality is why I expect fierce pushback from this proposal and difficulty passing it in Congress. When Synchrony announces Q4 results on Jan. 27, I would expect a strong defense of its current business practices.

An implemented cap poses significant downside risk

At 10%, lending to many consumers would no longer be profitable, and in response, I would expect companies like Synchrony to significantly curtail the amount of credit they offer. Credit card balances are currently just over $1 trillion (note the decline in 2025 was due to a technical change and not an actual drop in balances). Under a 10% cap, we could see balances compress by over 25%, in my view. My concern is that such an aggressive pullback in credit card lending could significantly reduce the spending power of consumers, leading to a decline in consumption and likely a recession, thereby also increasing net charge-offs.

This would be a worst-case scenario: banks would have worse net interest income, lower balances on which they earn interest, and higher credit losses. SYF’s tangible book value is ~$38. In such an environment, I would expect shares to migrate towards 1.5x tangible book value, or ~$57. There would also likely be a sharp reduction in buybacks, which have been a key focus of capital return policy, with its share count down 6.7% from last year. Given its CET1 capital ratio is an excellent 13.7%, I do view its 1.4% dividend yield as safe.

I would buy the dip, given I view the policy as unlikely to pass

Now, absent this policy, I see SYF earning $9.25-$9.50 in 2026, given my view that a recession is unlikely, meaning credit card losses should be similar to 2025. I have viewed up to 10x earnings as fair value, given the cyclicality of the business, or about $92.50 as the potential upside in shares. I also expect credit trends to improve in H1 2026, given what I expect to be a very favorable tax refund season.

At ~$80, shares have ~$13 of upside in my base case but ~$23 of downside in an adverse policy environment. This means the market is implying a ~36% probability of the downside scenario occurring for $80 to represent an appropriate breakeven price. Given such a policy would require congressional action in my view, and given the likely negative ramifications on the availability of consumer credit, I view passage as unlikely and below a 20% probability. As time goes on, I expect the pressure from this headline to pass and SYF shares to march back towards fair value. With over 12% upside to fair value, I would use this pullback to add shares and reiterate SYF as a "Buy."

I normally get my money back if I win. But the problem or situation is lawyers, advisors and so forth. There is a kind of unitary cost to that that if I can make a hundred million dollars isn't that different than if I can make a million dollars. So anything that involves eye contact, physical touch, actual interaction, of IRL human stuff, I think will become more and more dear and valued and important kind of live entertainment, live interaction, live community is what's going to be left. I hope and expect 10 years from now, 100 years from now, a thousand years from now, we survive a thousand years. And, and I don't think that that's going to be replicated.

Now you put AI in humanoid robots. you think how, especially with, I think one of the big kind of qualms I have is the obsequiousness of a lot of AI and how much people like it. And so it's like, if you could have really good humanoids that are like other people, except they're much smarter and they're much more impressed by you. I think that that could be a dark, future.

And so we'll see how we relate to one another, whether community and relationships can withstand that, you know, not kind of evil oppressive robot, but the impressed robot that gives you exactly, that can get inside your mind and like giving you exactly the answer that you want compared to another person.

If you look at broken marriages amongst the very tippy top of kind of the world's, you know, Forbes 400 list. There's something I don't, I'm not going to get the stat right. But if you look at the top 25 people there, there are many more marriages than people at the very top. And I think one of the things this represents is if you have this huge family office and you have dozens of people who are kind of their full time job is to tell you, you're great. You asked the perfect question and now I'm going to give you the perfect answer.

Thanks boss, fly you to wherever you want to. You have one person who talks back to you and that's your spouse. You're like this one person. Why won't they just do exactly what I want the second I want it like everybody else in my life? And that becomes intolerable. Well, eight billion people are about to be that multi-billionaire with a family office, at least on information. They won't fly you to Mallorca this weekend, but they'll talk to you about the thing you want to talk about in the tone that you want.

And that kind of obsequious impressed tone is I think one of the darker sides of AI, not because I hate it, but because I love it. And then you look at actual people who actually have a pot perhaps far more reasonable view of how great your idea was not being told to like chat GPT thought it was a great idea. So I think that that could be a huge problem.

Rena Sherbill: You think you're smarter than chat GPT, I doubt it.

Yeah, man. Chris, I really always enjoy talking to you. I think anybody that has listened to this show knows that I like a thoughtful analyst and you're at the top of the list. So really appreciate you taking the time and sharing with us.

Again, your company is Rangeley Capital. Your investing group is Sifting the World. You can find some free articles on Seeking Alpha.

Any final words that you want to leave or any way that people can get in touch with you?

Chris DeMuth Jr: Available on Seeking Alpha. think I've at least mentioned here or there. and, we'll certainly be following up this year on each of the specific ones, that I mentioned, Willis Lease, California First Leasing, Golden Entertainment.

And I have a kind of new best idea for 2026, recently revealed right at the end of December within Sifting the World. And that's going to be something that I kind of come out probably right at the end of this month. So we can circle back, or readers listeners can circle back in late January for my best idea for 2026 that is exclusive for this first month to within Sifting the World.

But it's a big investment of mine at Rangeley. It's going to have a very active month this month and very active quarter. So not a value trap.

I will be aggressively humiliated and embarrassed and impoverished or hopefully triumphantly enriched by this being either a dumb idea or a smart idea, consequences to be known shortly.

Rena Sherbill: Binary outcomes only.

Chris DeMuth Jr: Very binary.

Rena Sherbill: You are welcome back anytime to discuss that idea or any other idea. Open invitation. So thank you, Chris.

data.title: Franklin Growth Opportunities SMA Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: US equities delivered strong performance over the third quarter of 2025, buoyed, in part, by easing trade tensions and optimism surrounding artificial intelligence (AI). The US Federal Reserve’s (Fed’s) interest-rate cut in September also supported market sentiment, as did generally robust corporate earnings. Both the S&P 500 Index and the Nasdaq Composite Index ended the quarter at new record highs, while returns for the Dow Jones Industrial Average were more muted. In this environment, growth investing surpassed value in the large-capitalization tier, while it was the reverse in the mid- and small-capitalization segments, with small-cap stocks overall performing better than their large- and mid-cap counterparts.
  • Detractors: Both stock selection and allocation decisions worked against us relative to the Russell 3000 Growth Index benchmark. Stock selection and an overweight in the industrials sector; stock selection and an underweight in the information technology (IT) sector; and stock selection in the communication services sector were leading drivers of negative relative performance.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in the real estate sector offered modest relative returns, while the majority of positive relative returns were at the individual stock level.
  • Outlook: While headline risks continue to influence market sentiment, we think the bigger story for investors is technological innovations, particularly in artificial intelligence (AI). Advances in AI and related technologies are transforming industries and, in our view, creating growth potential for long-term investors. Innovative companies in sectors and industries across the economy are harnessing AI in their efforts to expand profit margins and drive revenue growth. We are seeing this in industries that deliver health care, manage logistics and more.

Performance Review

  • The strategy (pure gross of fees) underperformed the Russell 3000 Growth Index benchmark in the third quarter of 2025. The strategy also underperformed the index on a net-of-fee basis for the period. In the industrials sector, shares of law enforcement technology company Axon Enterprise were pressured by investor concerns about its $900 million acquisition of AI firm Prepared (not a portfolio holding). Despite Axon’s strong stock performance over the past year, many investors reacted negatively to the acquisition and potential integration risks, leading to a double-digit-percentage stock decline during the quarter. Despite the near-term underperformance, we view Axon as a beneficiary of AI and a key component to the digitalization of law enforcement and emergency services.
  • In the IT sector, an underweight in technology giant Apple was a relative detractor, but the position was a contributor in absolute terms. Apple has struggled to demonstrate how generative AI will be integrated into its ecosystem, but its share price rallied following a positive outcome from the Department of Justice on Apple’s relationship with Google (a subsidiary of portfolio holding Alphabet) and after the company announced plans to build a US manufacturing plant to help reduce its dependence on China and secure lower tariffs. In contrast, shares of mobile marketing platform operator AppLovin received a boost from its inclusion in the S&P 500 Index, benefiting returns in the IT sector.
  • Another relative contributor at the stock level was Roblox in the communication services sector. The online gaming platform operator reported strong bookings year-over-year in the second quarter, helped by improved algorithmic discovery and virality of games. We continue to have high conviction in the long-term growth prospects of Roblox and its ability to increase its market share in the global gaming market.

Outlook

  • While headlines have focused on trade tensions, elevated interest rates and fiscal uncertainty, both historical precedent and current market performance reinforce our core thesis that the enduring strength of US businesses and capital markets should help drive global economic growth into the next decade. We believe US companies and the broader economy continue to be powered by structural advantages and secular growth drivers.
  • In broad terms, US companies have pioneered breakthroughs that have reshaped industries—ranging from the internet and semiconductors to genomics and defense technologies. In our view, US companies are positioned at the forefront of generative AI, semiconductors, biotechnology and space exploration, creating powerful long-term tailwinds for investors seeking exposure to the world’s leading growth companies.
  • We see secular tailwinds that potentially support US market leadership in the years ahead. Generative AI and automation can unlock productivity and cost savings across sectors. Pro-business policies—such as infrastructure investment, deregulation and tax incentives—continue to foster domestic growth. Onshoring and supply-chain diversification are reviving domestic manufacturing and boosting output, particularly in the advanced industrial and energy markets.
  • In our view, economic conditions remain constructive for risk assets, with resilient growth, moderate inflation and broadly supportive monetary and fiscal policy. Corporate fundamentals also appear healthy, in our view, amid improved earnings revisions. Our strategy maintains a diversified approach designed to help us participate in ongoing market strength while remaining adaptable to shifts in market leadership.

data.title: Q4 2025 Earnings Preview: Can Corporate Profits Sustain S&P 500 Record Highs?
data.description:
The Q4 2025 earnings season for US corporations begins this week when big banks kick things off, starting with JPMorgan Chase (JPM) on Tuesday (Jan 13). With the benchmark S&P 500 closing at 6,966 on Friday due to stable jobs data, above its previous record high close of 6,944, investors will be looking at corporate earnings to assess whether the market’s momentum can continue in the new year.

Earnings growth in 2025 highlighted, once again, the resilience of the US economy, corporations and consumers. The Fed’s aggressive rate hikes in 2022 weren’t enough to dismantle markets in 2023 or 2024, just as the April 2025 trade shock wasn’t enough to derail US corporations last year. Despite thinking US companies would take on massive cost-cutting plans to offset higher tariffs, they were able, for the most part, to pivot production, renegotiate with suppliers, and pass costs onto consumers, leading to continued profit growth for the year.

Next week we’ll not only hear from US corporations as they wrap up 2025 with their Q4 results but get their much-anticipated outlooks for 2026.

What to Expect from Q4 Reports

The current consensus for Q4 S&P 500 EPS growth stands at 8.3%, according to FactSet. This would be the 10th consecutive quarter of growth for the index. Revenues are expected to have grown 7.7% YoY, the 21st consecutive quarter of growth and the second-strongest figure in three years.

Eight of the 11 S&P sectors are anticipated to post YoY gains, with Information Technology (25.9%), Materials (9.0%) and Financials (6.4%) leading the pack. Only Industrials (-0.5%), Energy (-1.7%) and Consumer Discretionary (-3.5%) are expected to post negative growth.1

Overall, 2025 EPS growth is expected to come in at 12.4%. For 2026, that figure is anticipated to improve to 14.9%.2

Big Banks Poised for Another Strong Showing

Financials are expected to be one of the leading sectors this season, as the environment for banks remains positive. Here is what investors should be looking for across the "Big Six" (JPM, MS, C, BAC, WFC, GS):

1) Dealmaking

The biggest story of Q4 2025 is the revival of Wall Street fees. Global M&A volume hit roughly $5 trillion in 2025, a massive 40%+ jump from the previous year.3 Wall Street Horizon tracked 473 M&A announcements in 2025, the highest amount since 2021. IPO announcements of 481 also hit a four-year high.

What to watch: Investment banking fees, especially from GS and MS. If they beat estimates here, it suggests the IPO and merger market is more or less thawed for 2026.

2) The Net Interest Income (NII) Pivot

With the Federal Reserve having cut rates to the 3.50-3.75% range in late 2025, the easy money from high interest rates is over.

What to watch: Specifically look for JPM and BAC management's guidance on NII. Investors want to see if loan growth (more people taking out mortgages/business loans at lower rates) is enough to offset the lower profit margins on those loans.

3) Regulatory Green Shoots

Following the 2024 election and recent shifts in D.C., the market is pricing in a lighter regulatory touch.

What to watch: Listen for mentions of capital requirements. If CEOs hint that they need to hold less capital, it means one thing: massive share buybacks and dividend hikes coming in 2026.

4) Consumer Health

Despite sticky prices and a softening labor market, the US consumer has continued to hang in there. Investors will be looking for signs on big bank calls that this can continue in 2026.

What to watch: Credit card delinquency data, specifically from Bank of America, is the best indicator of US consumer health. If delinquencies stay below 3%, the soft landing could officially be a reality.

(Source: Wall Street Horizon, Data as of December 8, 2026, Companies marked with an * are unconfirmed as of date listed prior.)

Q4 2025 Earnings Wave

The peak weeks of the Q4 earnings season are expected to fall between January 26 and February 27, with each week expected to see over 1,000 reports. Currently, February 26 is predicted to be the most active day, with 855 companies anticipated to report. Thus far, only 36% of companies have confirmed their earnings date (out of our universe of 11,000+ global names). The remaining dates are estimated based on historical reporting data.

(Source: Wall Street Horizon)

1 FactSet Earnings Insight, John Butters, January 9, 2026
2 FactSet Earnings Insight, John Butters, January 9, 2026
3 “Global M&A stages great rebound in 2025 with $4.8 trillion deal value to mark second-highest total on record,” Bain & Company, December 11, 2025
